question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) where the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH constant (0x81d0284f...) is verified to match keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)') — fuzz the struct encoding to confirm abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization) correctly encodes the bool isAuthorized field without padding ambiguity that could allow a different struct to produce the same hash, violating the invariant that the EIP-712 hash must uniquely identify the authorization intent, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization via hash collision? Proof idea: differential test encoding Authorization with isAuthorized=true vs false; assert distinct hashes.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who has obtained a signed Authorization with a far-future deadline (e.g., deadline = type(uint256).max) use it at any future time as long as the nonce has not been consumed, and if the victim cannot increment their own nonce (because they have no way to submit a no-op authorization), the signature remains valid indefinitely, violating the invariant that authorizations should be time-bounded and revocable, causing scoped impact: permanent unauthorized authorization grant capability? Proof idea: sign auth with deadline=type(uint256).max; warp far into future; assert still valid and executable.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who gains authorization over victim V via EcrecoverAuthorizer immediately call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(EcrecoverAuthorizer_address, false, V) to revoke EcrecoverAuthorizer's own authorization in Midnight, preventing V from using EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the attacker's authorization, creating a one-way lock where attacker retains authorization but V cannot use the sig-based path to revoke it, violating the invariant that exit paths must remain available, causing scoped impact: permanent unauthorized authorization and fund freeze? Proof idea: grant attacker auth; attacker revokes EcrecoverAuthorizer; assert V cannot use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke attacker.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization where authorization.authorized is the Midnight contract address itself, granting Midnight authorization over the authorizer's own account, and then exploit any Midnight function that calls itself (e.g., via multicall delegatecall) to act as the authorizer, violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized self-referential authorization enabling fund theft? Proof idea: set authorized=address(midnight); assert no Midnight function can exploit this to act on authorizer's behalf.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER exploit the fact that nonce[authorization.authorizer]++ in line 26 increments BEFORE the ecrecover and authorization checks, meaning a failed signature check still increments the nonce — verify whether the nonce is incremented before or after the require checks, and if before, whether an attacker can DoS a victim by submitting invalid signatures to burn nonces, violating the invariant that nonce consumption must correspond to successful authorization, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS of victim's authorization nonce sequence? Proof idea: submit invalid sig; check if nonce[V] incremented; assert nonce only increments on success.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER use Midnight.multicall to atomically (1) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(EcrecoverAuthorizer, true, attacker) to authorize EcrecoverAuthorizer for attacker's own account, (2) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with a pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=attacker, authorized=victim, isAuthorized=true) — note this would grant victim auth over attacker, not the reverse — confirm the authorization direction is correct and cannot be inverted, violating the invariant that authorization direction must match the signed intent, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization in wrong direction? Proof idea: verify Authorization struct field semantics; assert authorized cannot act as authorizer.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) signed by V, where V has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight, but between the signature creation and submission, V calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(EcrecoverAuthorizer, false, V) to revoke EcrecoverAuthorizer — confirm the submission now reverts because EcrecoverAuthorizer is no longer authorized in Midnight to call setIsAuthorized on V's behalf, and fuzz the timing window, violating the invariant that revocation of EcrecoverAuthorizer must immediately invalidate all pending signatures, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization grant if revocation window is exploitable? Proof idea: revoke EcrecoverAuthorizer; submit old sig; assert revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.take maker callback] Can an ATTACKER acting as a maker callback (onBuy/onSell) during Midnight.take call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to grant themselves authorization over the taker, then within the same callback call Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of the taker, violating the invariant that maker callbacks must not enable unauthorized state changes on the taker's account, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure from taker during take execution? Proof idea: malicious onBuy callback PoC; assert taker's collateral drained during take.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER fuzz the Authorization struct with authorization.authorizer == authorization.authorized == attacker and a valid self-signed signature, to grant attacker authorization over themselves (no-op in Midnight since isAuthorized[attacker][attacker] is set), then use this to confirm whether self-authorization creates any unexpected state (e.g., isAuthorized[attacker][attacker] == true enabling some bypass), violating the invariant that self-authorization must not enable any additional capabilities, causing scoped impact: unexpected authorization state enabling unauthorized actions? Proof idea: self-authorize; assert no additional capabilities beyond normal self-access.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who is a market creator create a market with a malicious enterGate that calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized during canIncreaseCredit/canIncreaseDebt checks to grant the attacker authorization over users who attempt to enter the market, then use that authorization to drain their funds, violating the invariant that gate checks must not enable unauthorized state changes on user accounts, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization grant via malicious gate callback? Proof idea: malicious gate PoC; assert attacker gains authorization over users who touch the market.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) where the EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH (0x47e79534...) is verified to match keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') — note the domain omits 'name' and 'version' fields present in standard EIP-712 — and confirm whether a wallet that signs a standard EIP-712 domain with name/version would produce a different digest, preventing accidental cross-domain replay, violating the invariant that the domain separator must uniquely bind signatures to this contract, causing scoped impact: cross-domain signature replay enabling unauthorized authorization? Proof idea: differential test standard vs minimal EIP-712 domain; assert distinct digests.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER exploit the fact that Midnight.multicall uses delegatecall (msg.sender preserved) to batch (1) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized granting attacker auth over victim V, and (2) Midnight.repay(market, units, V, attacker_callback) where the callback is attacker-controlled, to reduce V's debt without V's consent and redirect the repayment token pull to an attacker-controlled address, violating the invariant that debt reduction requires valid authorization and correct payer accounting, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt reduction and token theft? Proof idea: multicall PoC with repay callback; assert V's debt reduced and attacker controls token flow.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who obtains authorization over victim V via EcrecoverAuthorizer call Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, V) to cancel all of V's offers in a consumption group, then immediately take V's now-uncancellable offers at stale prices before V can react, violating the invariant that offer cancellation and fill must not be composable to extract value from the maker, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer cancellation followed by value extraction? Proof idea: grant auth; cancel V's group; assert V's offers cannot be filled at intended prices.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER fuzz the Signature struct with v values outside {27, 28} (e.g., v=0, v=1, v=29, v=255) to confirm ecrecover behavior — in Solidity 0.8.34, ecrecover with invalid v returns address(0), which is caught by require(signer != address(0)) — but fuzz all (v, r, s) combinations to confirm no non-zero address is returned for invalid v values that could match a real authorizer, violating the invariant that only valid ECDSA signatures must be accepted, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization via malformed signature? Proof idea: fuzz v in [0..255] excluding 27/28; assert all return address(0) or revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who is an authorized delegate D of victim V use EcrecoverAuthorizer to sign Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=D, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) to ensure D remains authorized, then sign Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=V_direct_key, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N+1) to revoke V's ability to call Midnight directly (note: Midnight checks onBehalf == msg.sender, so V can always call directly regardless of isAuthorized), confirm whether revoking isAuthorized[V][V] has any effect, violating the invariant that users must always be able to act on their own behalf, causing scoped impact: fund freeze if self-authorization revocation blocks direct access? Proof idea: revoke isAuthorized[V][V]; assert V can still call Midnight directly as msg.sender.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) where the hashStruct computation at line 28 uses abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization) — since Authorization contains a bool field, confirm abi.encode pads bool to 32 bytes and that the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH constant correctly reflects the canonical EIP-712 encoding, and fuzz struct field orderings to confirm no encoding ambiguity allows a different Authorization to produce the same hashStruct, violating the invariant that the EIP-712 hash must uniquely identify the authorization, causing scoped impact: hash collision enabling unauthorized authorization? Proof idea: differential fuzz Authorization encoding; assert no two distinct structs produce same hashStruct.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.withdraw] Can an ATTACKER use a signed Authorization to grant themselves authorization over a lender L who has credit in a market post-maturity, then call Midnight.withdraw(market, L.credit, L, attacker) to drain L's withdrawable loan tokens, violating the invariant that user funds must not be stolen outside valid protocol rules, causing scoped impact: direct theft of lender's post-maturity credit redemption? Proof idea: grant auth over L; warp past maturity; withdraw L's credit to attacker; assert attacker receives loan tokens.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who is a ratifier contract (authorized by maker M in Midnight via isAuthorized[M][ratifier]=true) sign an Authorization(authorizer=M, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) — note ratifier contracts typically cannot sign ECDSA — but fuzz whether any ratifier contract address could produce a valid ecrecover output for a crafted (v,r,s), violating the invariant that only EOAs or contracts with signing capability can produce valid ECDSA signatures, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization grant via contract-as-signer? Proof idea: fuzz (v,r,s) targeting ratifier contract address as ecrecover output; assert no valid sig exists for contract addresses.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER exploit the permissionless submission of setIsAuthorized to grief a victim by submitting a valid Authorization(isAuthorized=false) that the victim signed to revoke a delegate, but submitting it at a time when the victim needs the delegate active (e.g., during an automated strategy), causing the delegate's authorization to be prematurely revoked and the victim's position to become unmanageable, violating the invariant that authorization management must be under the authorizer's control, causing scoped impact: fund freeze due to premature authorization revocation? Proof idea: monitor mempool for revocation sigs; submit at critical moment; assert victim's strategy fails.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) where V has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight, and immediately in the same block use the new authorization to call Midnight.take(offer, ..., taker=V, ...) to force V into a borrowing position (sell offer) or credit position (buy offer) without V's direct consent, violating the invariant that every borrow/take must create correct debt and collateral obligation with valid taker authorization, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation for victim? Proof idea: grant auth; take sell offer on behalf of V; assert V.debt > 0 without V's intent.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER perform a stateful fuzz invariant test asserting that for all sequences of setIsAuthorized calls (via EcrecoverAuthorizer) and Midnight state-changing calls, the property midnight.isAuthorized(V, attacker) == true implies there exists a valid signed Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker) in the call history, and that no sequence of calls can set isAuthorized[V][attacker]=true without a valid signature from V or V's authorized delegate, violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization grant via any call sequence? Proof idea: Foundry invariant test with ghost tracking of signed authorizations; assert isAuthorized implies signed intent.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who controls a liquidator gate contract exploit the gate's canLiquidate callback to call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized during Midnight.liquidate execution to grant themselves authorization over the borrower, then within the same liquidation call Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of the borrower to seize additional collateral beyond the liquidation entitlement, violating the invariant that liquidation must not leave accounting desynced and collateral cannot be seized outside valid liquidation rules, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure beyond liquidation amount? Proof idea: malicious liquidator gate PoC; assert extra collateral seized beyond seizedAssets.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER perform a differential test comparing the authorization state after (A) calling Midnight.setIsAuthorized directly vs (B) calling EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with an equivalent signed Authorization, asserting that both paths produce identical isAuthorized[authorizer][authorized] state in Midnight, and that no additional side effects (e.g., nonce state, event emissions, intermediate state) differ in a way that could be exploited, violating the invariant that the sig-based path must be functionally equivalent to the direct path, causing scoped impact: authorization state desync enabling unauthorized actions? Proof idea: differential test both paths; assert identical Midnight state post-call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER who has obtained authorization over victim V via EcrecoverAuthorizer call Midnight.claimContinuousFee(market, amount, attacker) on behalf of V (if V is the fee claimer role) — note claimContinuousFee checks msg.sender == feeClaimer, not authorization — confirm that coarse-grained authorization does NOT grant access to role-gated functions, and fuzz all Midnight entry points to identify any that use isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender] where onBehalf could be a role holder, violating the invariant that role-gated functions must not be bypassable via authorization, causing scoped impact: unauthorized fee claim or role action? Proof idea: grant auth over feeClaimer; attempt claimContinuousFee; assert revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Unauthorized borrowing, withdrawal, collateral seizure, or debt reduction caused by broken authorization, signature, ratifier, gate, or callback logic'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an ATTACKER submit Authorization(authorizer=V, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) where the nonce check at line 26 uses post-increment (nonce[authorization.authorizer]++) meaning the nonce is incremented as part of the require evaluation — confirm that if the require fails (nonce mismatch), the nonce is NOT incremented (Solidity short-circuit), and fuzz edge cases where nonce overflow (nonce == type(uint256).max) could wrap to 0 and allow replay of nonce-0 signatures, violating the invariant that nonces must be strictly monotonically increasing without overflow, causing scoped impact: unauthorized authorization replay via nonce overflow? Proof idea: increment nonce to type(uint256).max; submit nonce-0 sig; assert overflow behavior.
